items.title: The Great AI Build-Out: Why Data Centers Became America's Biggest Construction Story
items.body:
Drive through the outskirts of Columbus, Phoenix, Atlanta, or northern Virginia in mid-2026 and you'll see the same thing: cranes, substations, and windowless buildings the size of shopping malls. The artificial intelligence boom has quietly become the largest private construction wave in modern American history — and it is reshaping the power grid, local politics, and the economy itself.

## The scale is hard to overstate

The major cloud and AI companies — Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta, and OpenAI with its partners — have publicly committed hundreds of billions of dollars to AI infrastructure, with multi-year projects like the Stargate initiative pitched in the half-trillion-dollar range. Combined annual capital spending by the hyperscalers now rivals the GDP of a mid-sized country. Much of it goes into physical things: land, concrete, cooling systems, fiber, and above all, electricity.

## Power is the new bottleneck

For roughly fifteen years, U.S. electricity demand was essentially flat. That era is over. Data centers have pushed demand onto a growth curve utilities haven't seen in a generation, and the scramble for power has produced deals that would have sounded absurd five years ago:

- **Nuclear revivals** — including the headline-grabbing plan to restart a reactor at Three Mile Island to feed Microsoft's data centers.
- **Small modular reactor (SMR) bets** from Amazon and Google, aimed at the early 2030s.
- **Natural gas turbines back-ordered for years**, as developers try to bring generation online faster than the grid can expand.

Interconnection queues — the waiting lists to plug new power into the grid — now stretch years in many regions, making electricity, not chips, the binding constraint on AI's growth.

## The local backlash is real

Communities hosting these facilities are asking harder questions in 2026 than they did in 2024. Data centers create enormous tax revenue but relatively few permanent jobs, consume significant water for cooling in some designs, and — most politically explosive — are increasingly blamed for rising residential electricity rates. Several states are debating rules that would force data center operators to shoulder more of the grid-upgrade costs they trigger.

## Why it matters beyond tech

Economists estimate that AI-related investment has become one of the biggest single drivers of U.S. GDP growth. That's a double-edged sword: it has cushioned the broader economy, but it also concentrates risk. If AI revenue fails to catch up with AI spending, the build-out slows — and the effects would be felt not just in tech stocks, but in construction payrolls, utility planning, and dozens of local economies that have bet their futures on the boom.

For now, the cranes keep rising. The second half of 2026 will offer the clearest evidence yet of whether the revenue is rising with them.

items.title: America at 250: How the Semiquincentennial Became the Cultural Event of 2026
items.body:
Two hundred fifty years after the Declaration of Independence was adopted in Philadelphia, the United States has just marked the biggest birthday in its modern history — and the celebration is far from over. The semiquincentennial (yes, that's the official word) is less a single holiday than a year-long cultural phenomenon, and this month is its high-water mark.

## Philadelphia at the center

The city where it all began has spent years preparing for this moment, and its Historic District — Independence Hall, the Liberty Bell, the Museum of the American Revolution — is running its most ambitious slate of exhibitions and public programming in decades. Fourth of July weekend brought the expected spectacle: massed fireworks, concerts, naval reviews, and tall ships in East Coast harbors, echoing the beloved Bicentennial imagery of 1976 for a new generation.

## More than fireworks

What makes 2026 culturally interesting isn't the pyrotechnics — it's the argument. Museums, theaters, and artists across the country are using the anniversary to ask harder questions than the Bicentennial did:

- **Museums nationwide** are staging exhibitions that put founding-era documents alongside the stories of women, Native nations, enslaved people, and immigrants — presenting 1776 as the beginning of an argument about liberty rather than its conclusion.
- **State and local 250 commissions** have funded thousands of community history projects, from oral-history archives in small towns to restored historic sites that had been closed for years.
- **Artists and playwrights** are revisiting the founding the way *Hamilton* did a decade ago — reinterpreting, contesting, and reclaiming it. Expect the fall arts season to be saturated with America-themed premieres.

## A mirror, not a museum piece

Anniversaries like this one tend to reveal more about the present than the past. The Bicentennial of 1976 arrived amid post-Watergate disillusionment and became, unexpectedly, a moment of grassroots healing. The semiquincentennial arrives in a similarly divided country, and the same question hangs over it: can a nation that disagrees about its past still celebrate it together?

The early evidence suggests Americans are answering in a characteristically American way — by showing up. Attendance at historic sites has surged all year, genealogy platforms report record interest, and "heritage travel" is one of 2026's defining tourism trends.

## If you want in

You don't need to be in Philadelphia. Nearly every state has a 250th-anniversary program running through the end of the year — check your state's America250 commission for local exhibitions, reenactments, and restored sites. The most memorable way to mark the anniversary may be the smallest: visit the courthouse, meetinghouse, or main street where history happened near you. That, after all, is where the story was always written.
